title: Long-range correlation and the spin conductivity in the XXZ chain from ballistic macroscopic fluctuation theory
abstract: Based on the ballistic macroscopic fluctuation theory, the integration of the spin correlation function (spin conductivity) is analyzed for the spin-1/2 XXZ chain in the critical regime. In the time when the magnetization of an infinite spin chain fluctuates from an initial state with a wavelength as long as the infinite length $N$, the equal-time two-point spin correlation function is scaled up to $O(1/N)$. In the state where the ballistic spin transport decays at high temperature $T$, the diffusive transport remains on a large scale. We show that the spin conductivity is proportional to $1/T$ in the limit $T\to\infty$ and its high temperature proportionality constant diverges in the case where one-quasiparticle magnetization is infinitely large. This analysis informs that the superdiffusive spin transport is driven by the $1/N$-scaled long-range spin correlation and sheds a light on the dynamic scaling in spin transport at the isotropic point.
summary: This paper uses Ballistic Macroscopic Fluctuation Theory to show that superdiffusive spin transport in the XXZ chain is driven by long-range spin correlations. It demonstrates that spin conductivity scales with 1/T at high temperatures and diverges at the isotropic point. This provides a theoretical link between microscopic correlations and macroscopic transport properties.
main_problem: Investigating whether superdiffusive spin transport in the XXZ chain is driven by 1/N-scaled long-range spin correlations and analyzing spin conductivity in the critical regime.
main_result: The study shows that spin conductivity is proportional to 1/T in the high-temperature limit and that its proportionality constant diverges as the system approaches the isotropic point, providing evidence for superdiffusive transport.
method: The author employs Ballistic Macroscopic Fluctuation Theory (BMFT) combined with Generalized Hydrodynamics (GHD) and the Thermodynamic Bethe Ansatz (TBA) to analyze fluctuations and correlations.
model_or_system: The spin-1/2 XXZ integrable spin chain in the critical regime (0 <= Delta < 1), characterized by an anisotropy parameter Delta = cos(theta).
key_observables: Spin conductivity, spin correlation functions, spin current density, magnetization density, and Drude weight.
important_parameters: Temperature (T), inverse temperature (beta), anisotropy parameter (Delta), and the thermodynamic limit (N -> infinity).
main_assumptions: Assumes the system is in the critical regime, relies on the integrability-driven existence of infinitely many conserved charges, and assumes local thermal equilibrium within fluid cells.
figures_summary: The figures illustrate the behavior of the conductivity constant sigma_0 as a function of the anisotropy parameter Delta, showing its decay at the free fermion point and its divergence as it approaches the isotropic point.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the model and the problem of long-range correlations, moves through the application of BMFT and GHD to calculate the evolution of correlations, derives the relationship between conductivity and correlation functions, and concludes with the high-temperature limit and scaling at the isotropic point.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to many-body dynamics as it provides a fundamental link between long-range correlations and anomalous (superdiffusive) transport in integrable systems, which is a central topic in non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
